catalyst.source: The reduction of CO 2 with water into valuable organic compounds under concentrated sunlight was investigated for the first time over a hybrid catalyst, in which a Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was combined with a Fe-based catalyst supported on a dealuminated Y-type zeolite (Fe-Cu-KDAY).
catalyst.context: The reduction of CO 2 with water into valuable organic compounds under concentrated sunlight was investigated for the first time over a hybrid catalyst, in which a Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was combined with a Fe-based catalyst supported on a dealuminated Y-type zeolite (Fe-Cu-KDAY).In this reaction system, the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 catalyst decomposes water to produce H 2 and the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst reduces CO 2 with the resulting H 2 into organic compounds.When only the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was used, organic compounds such as CH 4 , HCOOH, and HCHO were formed together with H 2 , indicating that CO 2 can also be directly reduced over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 .On the contrary, when the hybrid catalyst was used, CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were produced in addition to the above products, depending on the reaction temperature and catalyst composition.The reaction temperature significantly increased by concentrating the solar irradiation, reaching 600 K and increasing the product yields.It is suggested that such a high reaction temperature facilitates both the photoreduction and hydrogenation of CO 2 over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 and Fe-Cu-KDAY, respectively.

catalyst.source: The Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst with the composition of 1.0Fe:0.03Cu:0.5K:11.0DAY (weight ratio) was prepared by impregnation.
catalyst.context: The chemicals used in the present study were of reagent grade, purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Industry, and used without further purification.The dealuminized Y-type zeolite (DAY zeolite), with a SiAl ratio of 11.4, was prepared by steam dealumination of a commercial Y-type zeolite (Catalysts Chemicals Industry Co. Ltd., Japan), which was ion exchanged with NH 4 prior to use.The Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst with the composition of 1.0Fe:0.03Cu:0.5K:11.0DAY (weight ratio) was prepared by impregnation.DAY zeolite powders were impregnated in a mixed solution of metal nitrites at the designated ratio while stirring.The resulting powder samples were then dried overnight at 373 K, followed by calcination at 673 K for 2 h in a stream of O 2 at a flow rate of 30 mlmin, and subsequently reduced at 673 K for 2 h in H 2 (20 mlmin). The Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was synthesized as follows: 5.0 g of TiO 2 (P-25) powder was dispersed in distilled water containing 1.4 g of K 2 CO 3 at room temperature.The resulting samples were dried and then calcined at 1213 K for 20 h in air.Pt was loaded on the K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powder by photochemical deposition.3.0 g of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powder was dispersed in 60 ml of 2 M Na 2 CO 3 solution containing 0.019 g of H 2 PtCl 6 .The above suspension was exposed to the light of a 150 W Hg-lamp, resulting in the photochemical deposition of Pt on K 2 Ti 6 O 13 .Finally, the Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were thoroughly rinsed with distilled water. The prepared Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were physically mixed by grinding (hereafter, referred to as hybrid catalyst).The prepared catalysts were analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, RINT-1400) with Cu K radiation as well as a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, S-5200).Diffuse reflection spectra were recorded using a UV-Vis spectrometer (Jasco V-550).The BET surface area of the sample powders was measured by the nitrogen adsorption method. The photoreduction experiments of CO 2 with water under Xe-or Hg-lamp irradiation at room temperature were carried out using a photoreactor (173 cm 3 ) equipped with a 63.3 cm 2 flat quartz window.The catalysts were highly dispersed on a filter paper fixed on the bottom of the reactor, into which an appropriate amount of distilled water was introduced.The filter paper acted as a wet bed for the catalyst layer.After degassing using a vacuum system, highly purified CO 2 was introduced into the reactor, which was externally irradiated through the window by a 300 W Xe-lamp and a 150 W Hg-lamp, respectively. Fig. 1 shows a schematic drawing of the experimental system for the photoreduction of CO 2 with water under concentrated sunlight.As illustrated in Fig. 1, the prepared catalysts were placed on wet quartz wool in an optical quartz tube cell (reaction cell), and subsequently an appropriate amount of distilled water was introduced into the cell.In this case, the quartz wool was used as a wet bed for the catalyst layer.After the reaction cell was deaerated using a vacuum system, highly purified CO 2 was introduced into the cell.The cell was tightly closed using a perfluororubber O-ring and no organic contaminants were detected in blank tests performed without the addition of CO 2 .The cell was set on a support bar fixed on an equatorial mount (GPD-type, Vixen, Japan) driven by a sky sensor, which can track the sun automatically.In order to focus the sunlight on the catalyst layer, a concave mirror (diameter 200 mm) was also fixed on the support bar so that the catalyst layer was positioned at the focal point of the mirror.The reaction temperature was monitored with a thermocouple, which was connected to an NR-1000 data acquisition system (Keyence, Japan) and a laptop computer.The experiments were done between 9:30 and 15:30 h on sunny days.Solar insolation upon the reaction cell was measured with a pyronometer (Ishikawa Sangyou Co. Ltd., Japan) H 2 and CH 4 produced in the gas phase were determined using a gas chromatograph (GC) (Shimadzu, GC-14B) equipped with a molecular sieve 5 A column and detected by a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID), respectively.Ar was used as the carrier gas.HCOOH produced in the liquid phase was determined using a GC (Shimadzu, GC-6A) equipped with a PEG-6000 column and a TCD, while HCHO was determined using a Porapak T column and a TCD.CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were determined using a GC (Shimadzu, GC-17A) equipped with a capillary column and an FID.He was used as the carrier gas for the analysis of the products in the liquid phase.The amount of samples taken from the liquid phase for the GC analysis was 10 l.

catalyst.source: The reduction of CO 2 with water under concentrated sunlight over the hybrid catalyst, in which the Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was combined with the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, resulted in the formation of organic compounds such as CH 4 , HCOOH, HCHO, CH 3 OH, and C 2 H 5 OH.
catalyst.context: The reduction of CO 2 with water under concentrated sunlight over the hybrid catalyst, in which the Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was combined with the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, resulted in the formation of organic compounds such as CH 4 , HCOOH, HCHO, CH 3 OH, and C 2 H 5 OH.The formation of C 2 H 5 OH in this reaction system is worth noting, although the yield needs to be significantly improved.On the other hand, when only the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst was used in the absence of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst, no products were detected.This supports the idea that the H 2 resulting from the water decomposition over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst serves as the reducing agent for the CO 2 hydrogenation over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, indicating the effectiveness of the combination of the above two catalysts for CO 2 reduction.The reaction temperature reached 590 K by concentrating the solar radiation; such a high temperature promotes the CO 2 hydrogenation over the hybrid catalyst.It was also found that the activity of the photocatalyst for CO 2 reduction was improved under concentrated sunlight presumably by the simultaneous supply of photons and thermal energy.This study suggests that photocatalytic processes utilizing concentrated sunlight have the potential for organic compound syntheses from CO 2 and water without excessive energy. lamp.source: The above suspension was exposed to the light of a 150 W Hg-lamp, resulting in the photochemical deposition of Pt on K 2 Ti 6 O 13 .
lamp.context: The chemicals used in the present study were of reagent grade, purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Industry, and used without further purification.The dealuminized Y-type zeolite (DAY zeolite), with a SiAl ratio of 11.4, was prepared by steam dealumination of a commercial Y-type zeolite (Catalysts Chemicals Industry Co. Ltd., Japan), which was ion exchanged with NH 4 prior to use.The Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst with the composition of 1.0Fe:0.03Cu:0.5K:11.0DAY (weight ratio) was prepared by impregnation.DAY zeolite powders were impregnated in a mixed solution of metal nitrites at the designated ratio while stirring.The resulting powder samples were then dried overnight at 373 K, followed by calcination at 673 K for 2 h in a stream of O 2 at a flow rate of 30 mlmin, and subsequently reduced at 673 K for 2 h in H 2 (20 mlmin). Ltd., Japan) H 2 and CH 4 produced in the gas phase were determined using a gas chromatograph (GC) (Shimadzu, GC-14B) equipped with a molecular sieve 5 A column and detected by a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID), respectively.Ar was used as the carrier gas.HCOOH produced in the liquid phase was determined using a GC (Shimadzu, GC-6A) equipped with a PEG-6000 column and a TCD, while HCHO was determined using a Porapak T column and a TCD.CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were determined using a GC (Shimadzu, GC-17A) equipped with a capillary column and an FID.He was used as the carrier gas for the analysis of the products in the liquid phase.The amount of samples taken from the liquid phase for the GC analysis was 10 l.

operation mode.context: The chemicals used in the present study were of reagent grade, purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Industry, and used without further purification.The dealuminized Y-type zeolite (DAY zeolite), with a SiAl ratio of 11.4, was prepared by steam dealumination of a commercial Y-type zeolite (Catalysts Chemicals Industry Co. Ltd., Japan), which was ion exchanged with NH 4 prior to use.The Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst with the composition of 1.0Fe:0.03Cu:0.5K:11.0DAY (weight ratio) was prepared by impregnation.DAY zeolite powders were impregnated in a mixed solution of metal nitrites at the designated ratio while stirring.The resulting powder samples were then dried overnight at 373 K, followed by calcination at 673 K for 2 h in a stream of O 2 at a flow rate of 30 mlmin, and subsequently reduced at 673 K for 2 h in H 2 (20 mlmin). The Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was synthesized as follows: 5.0 g of TiO 2 (P-25) powder was dispersed in distilled water containing 1.4 g of K 2 CO 3 at room temperature.The resulting samples were dried and then calcined at 1213 K for 20 h in air.Pt was loaded on the K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powder by photochemical deposition.3.0 g of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powder was dispersed in 60 ml of 2 M Na 2 CO 3 solution containing 0.019 g of H 2 PtCl 6 .The above suspension was exposed to the light of a 150 W Hg-lamp, resulting in the photochemical deposition of Pt on K 2 Ti 6 O 13 .Finally, the Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were thoroughly rinsed with distilled water. The prepared Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were physically mixed by grinding (hereafter, referred to as hybrid catalyst).The prepared catalysts were analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, RINT-1400) with Cu K radiation as well as a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, S-5200).Diffuse reflection spectra were recorded using a UV-Vis spectrometer (Jasco V-550).The BET surface area of the sample powders was measured by the nitrogen adsorption method.
